data.title: Nebius Vs CoreWeave: Why Nebius Will Outperform
data.description:
Prelude

The neocloud industry has risen to prominence alongside commercial interest in AI. The term neocloud refers to a cloud service provider that is purpose built for AI. GPU-first data centers. Nebius Group (NASDAQ:NBIS) and CoreWeave (NASDAQ:CRWV) are two such neoclouds. In this article, I make the case that Nebius will outperform CoreWeave. Overall, Nebius’s debt‑free, vertically integrated model and favorable EV‑per‑GPU valuation make it the superior neocloud investment over CoreWeave.

Investing in either Nebius or CoreWeave represents a unilateral bet on continued strong demand for AI compute. This is a good bet. Hyperscalers have continued spending aggressively while the Trump Administration continues loosening AI-related regulations. Just today, the market applauded a flurry of announcements related to AI from the deal with the Saudis. Further, training frontier models continue to become more compute intensive:

Indeed, at a certain point we cannot simply throw more compute at models to make them smarter. The industry will need to adopt novel methods to train better models within capacity constraints. This will drive customers toward the most cost-efficient leading edge AI compute, which is Nebius. We'll return to this point later.

As the neocloud industry takes shape, the greatest opportunity comes from identifying the biggest winners early on. Both CoreWeave and Nebius are strong competitors here, but I believe Nebius stock will generate better returns over time. In this article, I rate CoreWeave a Sell and Nebius a Buy.

Why Nebius Will Outperform

Nebius stock offers a few distinct advantages over CoreWeave, in my opinion. First, the company's EU locality gives it a strong advantage in winning contracts in Europe and the Middle East due to data locality regulations like GDPR. Just today, the company was selected to build a supercomputer for the Israeli government. This EU locality will make it easier for Nebius to earn initial contracts to get started, providing sorely needed votes of customer confidence and revenue run rate to finance future GPU purchases.

CoreWeave's scale dwarfs that of Nebius: it has 250,000 GPUs currently versus Nebius' 30,000 and has 5x the Blackwell allocation. This is a double-edged sword. As Blackwell compute proliferates the market, the price of Hopper compute will continue sharply declining. These machines are still being depreciated, causing a huge overhang on earnings. Combined with CoreWeave's debt burden, the company has a much more difficult path to profitability than Nebius.

Nebius is expanding its overall GPU count by 73% with its 22,000 Blackwell GPUs while CoreWeave is expanding its count by 44% with its 110,000 Blackwell GPUs. Hopper depreciation will weigh more heavily on CoreWeave. Meanwhile, CoreWeave paid more in interest than it generated in operating income in 2024. The company's recent earnings release showed a similarly large overhang on earnings. We'll discuss that point later. Meanwhile, Nebius has no debt and earned $63m in interest income last year. Its path to profitability is much clearer than CoreWeave's.

Finally, valuation. I think a non-standard metric is best here: Enterprise Value to Blackwell capacity. To be a neocloud is to purchase the newest compute possible, operationalize it as quickly as possible, and earn as much money as it can before the next new platform is ramped. Therefore, it's best to evaluate the current enterprise value by the amount of Blackwell GPUs they have. This simplification ignores utilization and margin differences but serves as a clear value benchmark.

Nebius has an enterprise value of $6.53b using a share price of $36. It currently has about 22,000 Blackwell GPUs which it is accepting pre-orders for. This translates to a price of about $297,000 in EV per GPU.

CoreWeave's enterprise value is currently $37.6b using a share price of $62 and it has an allocation of 110,000 Blackwell GPUs. Using this proxy, you are currently paying about $342,000 in EV per GPU.

In essence, the difference in strategy (Nebius buying fewer GPUs but using cash instead of debt) allows investors to pay less per GPU for the stock. This is core as to why I believe Nebius stock offers better value than CoreWeave.

With that, let's dive a bit into each company's history, financials, and strategic approach.

Nebius

Nebius was borne out of the ashes of Yandex, a Russian tech conglomerate that managed some of the largest data centers in Europe. It is now headquartered in the Netherlands.

Former Yandex CEO Arkady Volozh, a Russian national that now lives in Tel Aviv, was placed on the EU sanctions list following the start of the Ukraine-Russia war and the company was sanctioned in the European market. Accordingly, Volozh publicly denounced the Russian invasion. Yandex assets were divested, raising over $2b in the process, that were then used to begin procuring Nvidia (NVDA) GPUs for the company's new life as Nebius.

As a result, the company's reported revenue dropped sharply from 2021 to 2022, and now sits at $117.5m. It achieved a 37% gross margin in fiscal 2024 on this revenue. For fiscal 2025, management believes it can achieve $750m-$1b in annual revenue run rate. The company has a very comfortable cash position of $2.49b as of the end of 2024 with an immaterial amount of debt. The company recently raised $700m in an oversubscribed offering that most notably included Nvidia.

The company's history is a double-edged sword. On one hand, the Russian association, even though management has distanced itself from the country and Yandex, could contribute to some reluctance from customers to sign contracts. On the other hand, the company is well-funded from the divestiture and has deep technical expertise that has allowed it to build a vertically integrated neocloud platform.

Nebius has the expertise to design its own data centers, procure its own compute, and deploy its own AI software layer. These in-house services greatly reduce the company's cost profile, allowing it to profitably offer low pricing.

Nebius' core business, AI infrastructure, currently maintains data centers in Finland, France, a pilot deployment in Kansas City, and an H200 cluster in Iceland. Most of the company's 30,000 GPUs are H200s, but it has secured a 22,000 Blackwell allocation from Nvidia. The Blackwell cluster in New Jersey is expected to be operational in 2025. This timeline is very achievable, and I expect a very positive update from management in the upcoming earnings call about this.

Nebius worked with European data center developer DataOne on this project. The CEO of DataOne shared this post on LinkedIn last week:

According to this post, the project is halfway done, and the infrastructure should be in place within the next 10 weeks. As another Seeking Alpha analyst recently pointed out, rental prices of Hopper GPUs have been declining, but I don't view this as strictly bearish for Nebius. Customers were hesitant to sign contracts for Hopper capacity in anticipation of the general rollout of Blackwell capacity across the industry. The stock will respond much more strongly to positive news about Blackwell capacity than it will respond to negative news about Hopper pricing.

Other Businesses

The core AI infrastructure business gives plenty of reason to be bullish on Nebius stock. There are strong structural tailwinds benefitting the company in this industry right now. Beyond this, Nebius has four other key points that provide a bullish backdrop for the stock in the remainder of 2025 and on. These other businesses are: Avride, Toloka, TripleTen, and ClickHouse.

Avride is an autonomous vehicle service that secured partnerships with Grubhub and Uber Eats (UBER) for autonomous food delivery services in 2024. Avride is fairly capital intensive for a non-core business, but it's done well to carve out a strong niche in the fast-growing autonomy market. It has great acquisition potential for Nebius.

Toloka is a data platform for AI training and grew sales by 140% in 2024. Toloka accelerates AI training timelines by providing important services like data labelling and red teaming for AI agents.

TripleTen is an educational tech service that helps re-skill workers for the AI era. Enrollments doubled in 2024.

Finally, the company has a 28% stake in ClickHouse, a database company in talks for a $6b funding round, valuing Nebius' stake at over $1.5b. The announcement of this funding round is what sent the stock powering forward last week.

Nebius offers direct exposure to the booming growth AI infrastructure sector and touts a set of ancillary businesses that could prove to be quite valuable over time. I particularly like the company's balance sheet, considering it has a comfortable cash pile and no debt. There won't be the overhang on earnings that high interest payments would cause, and the company can burn through its cash to accumulate GPUs before needing to rely on debt. These GPUs will become cash generative machines as the company works through the uptick in depreciation expense and then looks forward to its strategy for acquiring Nvidia's next-gen Rubin GPUs.

CoreWeave

CoreWeave has had a really strong five-year run. It transitioned from an Ethereum mining company to a neocloud after Ethereum began the shift to a proof-of-stake protocol. This was a logical transition considering it used Nvidia GPUs for its ether mining operations. It has a long-standing relationship with Nvidia and remains one of the company's leading neocloud partners.

CoreWeave garnered a lot of attention as the AI bull run found its legs. In 2022, it bought around $100m in H100s. It signed Stability AI as a customer and earned a $100m investment from Nvidia in 2023. It was valued around $2b in May of 2023 and raised $2.3b in debt using H100's as collateral in 2023 as well. It also spent $1.7b to acquire Weights & Biases, which moved the company up the value chain into AI development tools.

Just a year later, the company's valuation ballooned to $19b. It was one of the first cloud service providers to receive Blackwell racks. Finally, it planned to IPO. In its initial IPO filing, it generated 60% of its revenue from Microsoft (MSFT). The company also signed a $12b contract with OpenAI, which also allowed OpenAI to acquire $350m worth of shares during the IPO. The IPO was initially planned to raise $2.7b at a $23b valuation but later reduced the IPO size to $1.5b. The company finished 2024 with 32 data centers and more than 250,000 GPUs.

CoreWeave is the leading neocloud based on scale, GPU allocation, and customer wins. It has a deep relationship with Nvidia and exclusively offers Nvidia GPUs. This guarantees it will be able to continue getting leading edge chip allocation from the king. Despite this strong competitive positioning, the company's financial profile leaves a lot to be desired. In the company's Q1 earnings report, it reported $981m in sales, $561m in technology and infrastructure cost (which included $443m in depreciation), and $263m in interest expense. Despite the strong gross margin of 73%, depreciation expense was 45% of sales while interest expense comprised 27% of sales.

The company faced a 629% increase in technology and infrastructure costs in 2024, driven mostly by depreciation costs of Hopper GPUs. The Q1 report confirms this will continue to be a significant overhang on earnings.

The rapid growth of depreciation and interest expenses is the bear case for CoreWeave that is hidden in plain sight. These expenses aren't going anywhere. Nvidia is delivering new GPU platforms at a breakneck pace, with each successive generation offering significant performance improvements compared to the previous. The rollout of the Blackwell generation caused rental prices of Hopper compute to tumble down. This is going to be a consistent cycle for neoclouds, who will have to grapple with high depreciation costs for assets with a timestamp on their profitable lifetime. CoreWeave faces a double whammy of high depreciation costs and high interest costs while it will continue to rely on debt to acquire new GPUs.

With that, let's briefly discuss the final few key differences between Nebius and CoreWeave and I'll reiterate why I believe Nebius is set to outperform.

Key differences

Nebius built its entire platform from the ground up, while CoreWeave had to take on more debt to acquire its way up the value chain with the Weights and Biases acquisition. Considering the capital intensity of the neocloud industry and the fact that both of these companies have immaterial revenues compared to the capex required, I see Nebius as the far better option. The company has the expertise required to vertically integrate from data center and rack design up to the software layer. It has other businesses that have huge potential to accrue lots of value for shareholders.

Meanwhile, CoreWeave has several deep relationships with AI leaders. It is the leading neocloud partner for Nvidia and has close ties with Stability and OpenAI. This ensures its long-term relevance, but the company's strategy of taking debt to accumulate GPUs that quickly become obsolete makes a difficult path to profitability. Further, it had to acquire its way up the value chain (relying on more debt in the process), compared to Nebius' in-house vertical integration. By no means is CoreWeave irrelevant or not an innovator, I just believe that Nebius presents a far better opportunity at present.

Finally, on pricing. Nebius is positioning itself as the low-cost competitor while CoreWeave prefers to sign larger contracts with more premium pricing. As of May 10th, Nebius has the lowest publicly available rental cost for H200 GPUs at $2.30/hour (subject to certain terms), compared to the GetDeploying index. Nebius can offer industry leading prices profitably because its vertically integrated model provides much better unit economics. Handling data center design, OEM services, and software development internally has clear cost benefits to outsourcing these items. This should help the company overcome the initial hurdle of signing large customer contracts. However, Nebius' lack of customer wins relative to CoreWeave is the largest bearish factor in my argument. The company needs more customer wins in 2025 for the valuation to continue expanding.

Investor Takeaway

The neocloud industry is set to broadly outperform as more AI workloads shift to Blackwell chips. Despite the decline in Hopper prices, the shift in focus toward Blackwell provides a compelling setup for a refresh in neocloud growth outlooks and a rerating of valuation multiples.

Both Nebius and CoreWeave are neoclouds seeking to satiate the strong global demand for AI compute. While CoreWeave will have a much larger GPU footprint and Blackwell allocation, Nebius offers a more diversified business, more value-added services, and the agility of being debt-free.

Nebius does not yet have the brand-name recognition of CoreWeave nor the customer wins, forming the key bearish risks to my thesis, but the business is well positioned for growth. If Nebius fails to convert its Blackwell ramp into material contracts by the end of 2025, its valuation could contract materially.

However, I expect the company will either reiterate or raise the $750m-$1b expected annual revenue run rate guidance for year-end 2025 given the solid progress of its Blackwell buildout in the US. Further, the company's other businesses provide enormous upside potential that do not currently look priced into the stock. For these reasons, I expect Nebius will outperform CoreWeave. I rate CoreWeave a Sell and Nebius a Buy.

data.title: April U.S. Trade Prices: Cool Energy Prices Hide Gains In Durable Goods Trade Prices
data.description:
The U.S. trade indexes both came in above expectations, posting slight gains over the estimates that suggested both import and export prices would fall in April. Durable goods segments were the key to the upside beats in both indexes as scramble to build inventories ahead of elevated tariff rates and potential retaliation drove trade inflation.

Import Prices

U.S. import prices increased 0.1% MoM and 0.1% YoY in April, beating expectations of a -0.4% MoM decline. The forecast must have anticipated that the continued decline in energy import prices would overwhelm rising prices in other imports, but that wasn’t the case. Fuel imports did see another sharp decline last month, falling -2.6% MoM after the -3.4% MoM decline in March, but that was offset by a 0.4% MoM increase in nonfuel imports, the largest increase since a year ago and the 3rd largest since December 2022. The details in the nonfuel segment suggest tariffs played a role in increasing import prices:

  • The overall industrial supplies import price index declined -0.5% MoM, but when stripping out fuels, the index actually jumped by 0.8% MoM. This includes a 1.5% MoM increase in durable industrial supplies & materials. The largest increases were seen in nonmetals (+2.6% MoM) and finished metals (+2.4% MoM) related to durable goods, with the latter index up 12.7% YoY. Finished metal goods are one of the largest US imports from Canada, so the increase in the last three months there (averaging 1.8% MoM) is likely tariff-driven.

  • The prices of various types of finished imported goods were also on the rise in April. Capital goods import prices increased 0.6% MoM, the largest monthly gain since January 2022, and consumer goods (ex-autos) import prices increased 0.3% MoM. Auto import prices ticked up 0.2% MoM, a sneaky six-month high. Within each type of good, the durables subsegments appear to be the hottest. Nonelectrical machinery, a capital goods subsegment that has a relative importance of over 22% and covers most capital goods, increased 0.6% MoM, the largest gain since January 2022.
  • The cooler segments in import prices were energy, as mentioned above, and food & beverages, which saw no growth in April. Notable subsegments in these softer areas were imported natural gas prices (-17.5% MoM) and nonagricultural foods (-0.4% MoM).

While fuel import prices have kept the headline import price index in check, there was a noticeable trend of higher import prices for durable goods, which drove the beat over expectations in April. This includes sizeable increases in both the supplies to the U.S. durable goods manufacturing sector and the imports of finished capital and consumer durable goods. While there are some signs of de-escalation by the Trump administration, effective tariff rates are still going to be higher than they were before his second term, so we should expect that import price gains in the medium-term (6-month, 12-month rates) will accelerate.

Export Prices

U.S. export price growth in April also beat expectations, rising 0.1% MoM when a -0.5% MoM drop was expected. The increase was seen across both agricultural and non-agricultural exports, up 0.5% MoM and 0.1% MoM, respectively. Just like in import prices, energy continues to have a noticeable impact as export prices actually increased 0.8% MoM if fuels and foods are excluded, the largest monthly increase for that segment in three years. Export prices were also similar to import prices in that durable goods segments were the hottest segments, offset by cooler readings in nondurables.

The durables segments that saw the largest increases in April had smaller weights in the headline index. The prices of industrial supplies & materials for durable goods jumped 2.9% MoM, the largest gain since March 2022, and were up 11.5% YoY. Export prices for finished durables like auto vehicles & parts and non-auto consumer durable goods increased 0.5% MoM and 0.8% MoM. Inflation in the prices of exports to the primary metal manufacturing industry was particularly hot, up 4.3% MoM and 25.7% YoY. Overall, the data support the narrative that other countries were front-running potential “Liberation Day” retaliation measures in April.

Market Reaction

While there was some heat visible in the April update of import and export prices, the market did not see it as a reason to trade rates higher. The yield curve was broadly lower an hour after the trade price data was released, with the 2-year Treasury rate down -2 bps and the 10-year Treasury rate down -3 bps. Investors might have opted for a more dovish trade in response to weaker-than-expected housing starts and building permits, a report that tends to have more impact than import/export prices. U.S. equity prices generally trended lower following the release. S&P futures (SPX) erased all of the morning gains, and SPY and QQQ both eventually opened basically flat. The dollar index (DXY) was more volatile, but overall is flat on the day.

Like the PPI data yesterday, pockets of the import and export price data have begun to rise in response to tariffs, but from a bird’s eye view, there is not a major inflationary theme. The most comprehensive themes from today’s report are weakness in energy trade prices and strength in durable goods trade prices. The latter can become a short-term trend if trade de-escalation continues, but if not, trading with the U.S. is likely to become more expensive as tariff costs are spread out across the supply chain. For now, the movement is just a warning sign to consider as more post “Liberation Day” hard data is digested by the markets.

*Cumulative Total Returns

Key Takeaways

  • Portfolio performance was negative over the last quarter, driven by uncertainty around global macroeconomic factors and political developments that led to significant global market volatility. Despite Bitcoin (BTC-USD) reaching a historic price milestone, the overall market experienced a pullback, with significant declines in major indices.
  • Over the course of the quarter, U.S. institutional investment into the digital assets market continued to rise, with inflows into Bitcoin ETPs and the filing of several new crypto ETPs. Strategic steps by the Trump Administration and several U.S. state governments to establish cryptocurrency reserves aim to position the U.S. as a leading crypto hub.

Market Overview

US stocks experienced heightened volatility during the first quarter amid concerns about Trump's tariff policies and their impact on economic growth, inflation and the US Federal Reserve's (Fed's) interest-rate policy, with some investors fearing a potential recession or stagflation (stagnant economy and high inflation).

Underwhelming earnings reports and guidance from some companies and significantly lower consumer confidence also hindered sentiment. Despite reaching new closing highs earlier in the quarter, the Dow Jones Industrial Average (DJI) and the S&P 500 Index (SP500, SPX) ended the quarter with negative returns. The technology-heavy NASDAQ Composite Index (COMP:IND) suffered a significantly larger decline than the other two indexes as investors remained concerned about the potential effects of a Chinese company's new artificial intelligence model on US companies' AI spending. The Fed kept the federal funds target rate unchanged at its January and March meetings after cutting it three times in 2024 for a total of 100 basis points. At the press conference following the March meeting, Fed Chair Jerome Powell indicated that the central bank's forecast of weaker economic growth and higher inflation in 2025 generally balanced out each other, thus leading the Fed to leave the March forecast of approximately 50 bps in rate cuts for this year unchanged from its December 2024 projection.

Annual inflation, as measured by the core personal consumption expenditures price index-which excludes volatile food and energy prices and is the Fed's preferred inflation gauge-rose at a higher-than-expected rate in February after cooling in January, remaining well above the Fed's 2% long-run target. Meanwhile, the core Consumer Price Index (CPI) rose at a lower-than-expected annual rate in February and reached the lowest level since April 2021. Headline CPI cooled in February after rising in January at the fastest month-over-month rate since August 2023 and the fastest annual rate since June 2024.

The US labor market remained resilient despite economic uncertainty. While the number of new jobs added in February was below consensus expectations, it was higher than the downwardly revised figure for January; meanwhile, the unemployment rate ticked slightly higher in February after edging lower for two consecutive months. The country's gross domestic product expanded in 2024's fourth quarter at a lower annualized rate than in the previous quarter as growth in consumer spending and government spending was partially offset by a decline in investment.

Manufacturing activity contracted in March after expanding for two consecutive months as new orders, production and employment declined, with the prices subindex reaching the highest level since June 2022 due to higher raw materials prices due to tariffs. In contrast, services activity expanded for the ninth consecutive month in March but at a slower pace. Growth in business activity partially offset a decline in the employment subindustry, which slipped into contraction territory.

Stock markets worldwide remained turbulent over the period and major US stock indexes declined. The S&P 500 Index, DJIA and NASDAQ Composite Index dropped −4.27%,−0.87% and −10.26%, respectively. Four out of the 11 S&P 500 sectors finished with negative returns with consumer discretionary and information technology selling off the most. Conversely, the energy sector was a bright spot for performance as investors turned more defensive. Small-cap stocks struggled over the quarter followed by large- and midcap equities. In all three tiers, value stocks outperformed growth, with the large-cap tier ending modestly positive.

The first quarter of 2025 brought generally negative performance for cryptocurrencies and broader digital assets. The total crypto market cap decreased by 27.19% over the quarter. The S&P Cryptocurrency LargeCap Index, which tracks the largest and most liquid cryptocurrencies, fell by 20.75%, while the S&P Cryptocurrency MegaCap Index, focused on the two largest cryptocurrencies by market capitalization, Bitcoin and Ethereum (ETH-USD), fell by 17.72.

This market pullback was largely due to global macro activity and political developments. The correlation between most traditional finance (TradFi) sectors and digital assets, declined over the quarter. The correlation between digital assets and commodities experienced negative or neutral correlations, oil and gold experienced slightly negative and slightly positive correlations respectfully with digital assets, and the correlation between digital assets and equities saw a slight increase.

Sources: Santiment, Seeking Alpha, FTDA Resources.Representative benchmarks: USD (Bloomberg Dollar Spot Index), Oil (Dow Jones Commodity Crude Oil Total Return Index), Commodities (S&P GSCI Total Return CME), ETH (S&P Ethereum Index), BTC (S&P Bitcoin Index), Crypto Large Cap (S&P Cryptocurrency LargeCap Index), BTC/ETH (S&P Cryptocurrency MegaCap Index), S&P 500 (S&P 500 Index), NASDAQ 100 (NASDAQ 100 Index), US Bonds (Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Index), Gold (Bloomberg Composite Gold Index). For illustrative purposes only. Diversification does not guarantee a profit nor protect against loss.

On Sunday January 9th, Bitcoin reached yet another historic price milestone, hitting an all time high of $109k. However, BTC would still close the quarter down at $71k. Ethereum, however, failed to reach Q4 ' 24 highs during the same time period and experienced a notable decline over the quarter, even relative to BTC. As a result, ETH/BTC - the price of ETH in BTC dropped to a new local low of 0.02168 during the quarter, a level not seen since 2020, highlighting Ethereum's continued underperformance relative to Bitcoin in both up trending and down trending markets. Conversely, Bitcoin dominance (Bitcoin's % market capitalization of total crypto market capitalization) soared to a local high of 62.48%, reaching levels not seen since 2021. This underscores the broader risk-off sentiment where capital rotated back into BTC from smaller cap tokens, given BTC's sentiment as a store of value asset. Institutional investment in cryptocurrencies continued to rise during Q1. Spot Bitcoin ETPs brought in $870M in net inflows for the quarter, while Ethereum ETPs saw $253M of net outflows. Additionally, Institutional interest in new cryptocurrency spot ETFs rose, as product offering filings expanded beyond Bitcoin and Ethereum and entered new horizons. New ETP filings included spot ETFs for Solana (SOL-USD), Ripple (XRP-USD), Polygon (MATIC-USD) and Avalanche (AVAX-USD), as well as hybrid multi-token ETPs.1

Although both traditional and crypto markets experienced volatility with the new administration's tariff policies, regulatory sentiment shifted in favor of digital assets. The Trump Administration took tangible steps towards establishing a strategic U.S. Bitcoin reserve, as well as a United States Digital Asset Stockpile, both of which would incorporate digital assets to the United States balance sheet on a federal level.2 A March press letter laid out a strategy of diversifying national asset holdings while minimizing market disruption, which would be done through seizing cryptocurrency holdings rather than increasing taxpayer burden. A committee and interagency task force would manage the reserve to ensure ongoing regulatory compliance. Similarly, Trump appointed Paul Atkins, a pro-crypto lead with notable personal digital asset holdings, as the head of the Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC).3 This appointment set the stage for the release of the GENIUS act, a congressional bill outlining the regulation and payment of stablecoins. This bill proves to be a large step in the widespread rollout of digital assets on a wider level.4

Many local state governments also took steps towards establishing state-level cryptocurrency reserves. Specifically, Texas passed a bill to establish a state managed Bitcoin reserve, while Utah enacted legislation prohibiting state and local government entities from restricting the acceptance or custody of digital assets. Over the quarter, 10+ US states either pushed new or furthered proposed legislation for a statewide digital asset reserve policy. These announcements come at a timeline parallel to that of Trump's Bitcoin strategic reserve by the US government to re-establish the US as the crypto capital.5

Portfolio Positioning

Franklin Templeton Digital Assets Dynamic Bitcoin/Ethereum seeks to provide excess return or alpha over a market capitalization weighted benchmark of Bitcoin and Ethereum through long only exposure to BTC and ETH. The Franklin Templeton Digital Assets investment team determines weights for BTC and ETH based on fundamental research, which includes our proprietary tokenomics scoring system, and establishes relative risk and return of each digital asset.

Portfolio Highlights

For the first quarter of 2025, Franklin Templeton Digital Assets Dynamic Bitcoin/Ethereum performance was −17.50% (net of fees). On an absolute basis both the currency and smart contract platform sectors contributed to negative performance.

During the first quarter of 2025, we maintained our relative value-based investment strategy, which meant we sustained our portfolio weights from our last rebalance in Q4 of 2024, despite market fluctuations. The portfolio was underweight in Ethereum entering the quarter, which was a view maintained throughout the quarter as the price of ETH continued to decline. This decision was influenced by the ongoing challenges faced by Ethereum, including the relatively low transaction throughput and declining on-chain activity.

Additionally, net outflows into Ethereum ETPs further dampened investor confidence. In contrast, Bitcoin demonstrated relative resilience over the quarter, supported by strong investor sentiment and positive inflows into Bitcoin ETPs. Institutional demand for Bitcoin as a strategic reserve asset, both by the U.S. government and globally, reinforced its position. We remained slightly underweight in Ethereum and overweight in Bitcoin.

Our dynamic strategy enables us to swiftly adapt to market developments and optimize our investment outcomes. The portfolio is index-controlled and traded in a risk-oriented manner, with trades scaled based on relative value and fundamental research. As the market evolves and activity/adoption occurs between the two networks, we continue to re-evaluate our allocations to Bitcoin and Ethereum.

Bitcoin (BTC) 89.97

Ethereum (ETH), in the smart contract sector, is a decentralized, smart contract blockchain with its own cryptocurrency. Founded in 2013, Ethereum allows for the use of smart contracts and the ability to build decentralized applications. Ethereum, unlike Bitcoin's PoW, is a proof-of-stake (PoS) blockchain. PoS is a consensus mechanism that uses validators to produce and approve blocks of transactions. Over the quarter, Ethereum wasn't exempt from macroeconomic uncertainties facing the larger markets. An overall lack of activity on the Ethereum network hit the price of ETH hard; despite being added to the U.S. strategic reserve initiative, it experienced a general decrease in transaction throughput. ETHs fundamental challenges in on-chain activity were compounded by a broader market sell-off in risk that negatively impacted all digital assets. Even after Ethereum switched to Proof-ofStake, changing its underlying model from an energyintensive one to a more economical and adaptable alternative, there are still shortcomings in its fee revenue accrual, reflected by ETH price. The $30M in total fees generated by Ethereum transactions in March 2024 dropped substantially to around $500k in March 2025.6 ETH had a negative contribution to the portfolio in Q1.

Bitcoin (BTC), in the currency sector, is the original cryptocurrency. Originally proposed by the alias Satoshi Nakamoto in a paper in 2008, Bitcoin is the first application of blockchain's peer-to-peer network and distributed digital ledger to create a network for decentralized digital currency. The protocol uses the proof-of-work (PoW) consensus mechanism to secure its network through a cryptographic mathematical puzzle that validates transaction data on the chain. Bitcoin faced its worst Q1 in a decade, as the economic uncertainty of the new presidential administration largely contributed to a significant BTC selloff.7 Specifically, U.S. tariff policy contributed to larger risk on uncertainty within the markets. However, the establishment of a strategic Bitcoin reserve sparked institutional interest, as U.S. government demand signaled growing legitimacy and served as a strong indicator of Bitcoin's future utility. BTC was a negative contributor this quarter.

Digital Asset and Strategy Outlook

The first quarter of 2025 was a challenging period for digital assets, marked by significant market pullbacks and evolving political developments. The S&P Cryptocurrency LargeCap Index fell by 20.75%, while the S&P Cryptocurrency MegaCap Index declined by 17.72%. Despite Bitcoin reaching a historic milestone of over $109k, Ethereum experienced a notable drop, leading to a shift in market cap dynamics favoring Bitcoin over other lower market cap altcoins. Institutional investment in cryptocurrencies continued to rise, manifesting itself through substantial inflows into Bitcoin ETPs and new ETF filings for various cryptocurrencies. The Trump Administration and several U.S. state governments took strategic steps to establish cryptocurrency reserves, aiming to position the U.S. as a leading crypto hub. These efforts included legislative actions and the creation of task forces to manage and regulate digital asset holdings, setting the stage for potential future growth and stability in the digital assets market.

We have assessed the long-term value of blockchain technology and believe it will be foundational for the future of the asset management industry. Our CEO Jenny Johnson believes that blockchain technology has the potential to be a massive disruptor for traditional finance. In our opinion, digital assets are a major theme on which investors will become increasingly focused on gaining exposure to in their investment portfolios in the years ahead. We define digital assets as frontier risk alternatives - new investible opportunities that capture the return streams of novel business models and can generate long-term growth. We believe that as companies continue to develop new technologies on blockchain rails, the economics and value created can be captured through strategic selection of these protocol's underlying tokens.

Investment Team

Anthony Pecore, Senior Vice President, Director, Digital Asset Management

Twelve investment team members who are involved in fundamental and quantitative research, data science, and trading.

data.title: Tencent's Margins, Momentum, And Moat: A Bull Case Building Quietly
data.description:
If you are familiar with my previous articles, you probably know that I am rather bullish about a few Chinese technology companies – including the multinational technology conglomerate Tencent Holdings Limited (OTCPK:TCEHY). In my last article, which was published on February 23, 2025, I argued that a new bull market is taking shape and was rather optimistic about Tencent as an investment.

In the meantime, the stock gained about 3% in value, which sounds not like much but is actually resulting in an annualized return of 13% (not including dividends). We could also say that Tencent is trading almost for the same price as about three months earlier. But in the meantime, the stock declined rather steeply – similar to many other stocks in early April.

On May 14, 2025, Tencent reported first quarter results and aside from looking at the most recent metrics, we will also look at geopolitical developments of the last few months and what implications this might have on Tencent.

Quarterly Results

We start by looking at the first quarter results and revenue increased from RMB 159,501 million in Q1/24 to RMB 180,022 million in Q1/25 – resulting in a growth rate of 12.9% YoY growth for the top line. Operating profit also increased 9.5% year-over-year from RMB 52,556 million in the same quarter last year to RMB 57,566 million this quarter. And finally, diluted earnings per share increased from RMB 4.387 in Q1/24 to RMB 5.129 in Q1/25 – resulting in 16.9% year-over-year bottom line growth.

When looking at the three different business segments, all three contributed to growth. FinTech and Business Services, which struggled a bit in the last few quarters, grew 5.0% year-over-year to RMB 54,907 million in quarterly sales. Marketing Services increased its revenue from RMB 26,506 million in Q1/24 to RMB 31,853 million in Q1/25 resulting in 20.2% year-over-year growth. And finally, Valued Added Services, which is responsible for the biggest part of revenue and grew 17.2% year-over-year to RMB 92,133 million in Q1/25.

Aside from the growing revenue and growing earnings per share, Tencent also improved its operating margin from 36.8% in the same quarter last year to 38.5% in this quarter and gross margin improved from 52.6% in the same quarter last year to 55.8% this quarter. And when looking at the three different segments, each one improved its gross margin – especially FinTech and Business Services improved its margin from 45.6% in the same quarter last year to 50.3% in this quarter.

When looking at further metrics, combined monthly active users of Weixin and WeChat increased from 1,359 million in the same quarter last year to 1,402 million in this quarter – resulting in 3.2% year-over-year growth. The number of fee-based VAS paying users also increased 3.1% year-over-year from 260 million at the end of March 2024 to 268 million at the end of March 2025.

Growth

When looking at revenue growth, we see Tencent accelerating its top line growth in the recent past. In the last five quarters, Tencent constantly reported higher growth rates – from only 6% top line growth (which is still solid) to 13% top line growth in Q1/25.

And I don’t know if Tencent will be able to grow with an even higher pace in the coming quarters. But considering that China is still struggling and GDP growth in China still not back to levels before COVID-19, the revenue growth of Tencent is already high.

One way for Tencent to grow (right now and in the foreseeable future) is by investing heavily in AI. Tencent for example upgraded its advertising platform and is offering now enhanced generative AI capabilities – this is including ad generation, video editing tools or digital human solutions to facilitate live streaming for content creators and merchants.

Tencent is also rolling out several AI features for Weixin – similar to Meta Platforms (META), which is also rolling out Meta AI to be included in WhatsApp. During the last earnings call, management provided updates on this process:

In addition, we're in the early stages rolling out AI features within Weixin. Users can now add Yuanbao as a Weixin context for seamless AI interaction within Weixin Chat, providing context-aware responses and facilitating content discovery while leveraging the Weixin ecosystem and the World Wide Web. Weixin Search is now starting to include results powered by large language models including the fast thinking model HunYuan Turbo S and the Chain of Thought reasoning models HunYuan T1 and DeepSeek R1. We provide AI tools so that content creators can generate images matching the text of their official accounts articles and generate video effects for video accounts videos utilizing preset templates. We reduced Mini Programs development time via an AI coding assistant for creating AI programs that support natural language prompts and image inputs.

Another growth opportunity for Tencent are International Games and revenue in the first quarter of fiscal 2025 grew 23% year-over-year from RMB 13.6 billion in the same quarter last year to RMB 16.6 billion in this quarter. And not only International Games are growing with a high pace – Domestic Games grew 24% year-over-year from RMB 34.5 billion in the same quarter last year to RMB 42.9 billion in this quarter. However, it is worth mentioning that the last quarter was a “low-base quarter” and the high growth rates might be a bit misleading.

I would also assume that the FinTech and Business Services segment might contribute to high growth rates in the years to come. Right now, especially FinTech is rather struggling:

Our total commercial payment volume has been choppy in recent quarters and declined slightly year-on-year in the first quarter but improved in April. To support our wealth management initiatives, we integrated HunYuan and DeepSeek large language model capabilities into our financial assistant chatbot.

While FinTech was rather struggling, business services contributed to solid revenue growth:

Business services revenue returned to a teens year-on-year growth rate in the first quarter, benefiting from higher cloud services revenue as well as increased technology service fees generated from rising e-commerce transaction volumes. Business Services gross margin rose year-on-year to improved efficiencies.

Over the long run, I am optimistic that Tencent will be able to grow its FinTech business with a solid pace. The trend towards digital payments will continue and with the economic situation improving in the coming years, customers might also spend more leading to a higher payment volume.

Aside from growing its top line by investing in AI and other growth strategies, the company might also profit from an improving gross and operating margin in the years to come. I already mentioned above, that both margins improved year-over-year. When looking at the non-IFRS operating margin in the last three years, we see huge improvements. And although margins probably won’t improve with a similar pace as in the last three years, we can assume that margin improvements might contribute 2% or 3% to bottom line growth.

Additionally, share buybacks will also contribute to bottom-line growth. In the last few years, Tencent used share buybacks and although the company is not buying back shares with an aggressive pace, the number of outstanding shares was decreased from 9,704 million in 2021 to 9,261 in the first quarter of fiscal 2025 (the chart below is obviously not updated). When talking about share buybacks, we can also mention that Tencent had RMB 205,253 million in cash and cash equivalents on March 31, 2025, as well as RMB 132,549 million in term deposits on its balance sheet. If Tencent used the cash it has, it could repurchase about 640 million shares (about 7% of shares)

When summing up all the different strategies and contributors to growth, we can assume growth rates in the double-digits in the years to come. Analysts are also quite optimistic for the next few years and assume earnings per share will grow with a CAGR of 11.62% between fiscal 2024 and fiscal 2029.

Great Business

I already mentioned above that margins are improving again in the last few years. But when looking at the last ten years, we see that margins were already higher a decade ago. This should also make us optimistic that Tencent might be able to improve its margins further.

The picture is not perfect and Tencent’s margins were certainly fluctuating making it not the best example for stability and consistency. Nevertheless, the improving margins in the last few years are a good sign for the business.

Aside from the margins we are also looking at return on equity as well as the return on invested capital. And RoIC also declined in the last ten years a little bit, but the average RoIC in the last ten years was 17.46% and in the last 5 years, return on invested capital was still 16.73% on average. And as we can see in the following chart, a return on invested capital of 17% is not completely off the charts, but it is certainly a high RoIC and a number many businesses can’t achieve – especially not over the long run.

We can also look at the stock performance over the last 15 years and see an impressive outperformance of Tencent versus the S&P 500.

Of course, the stock performance is always a bit tricky as stocks can over- and underperforman for a long time – meaning that the stock price can be disconnected from the fundamental business performance for 5 or 10 years (or sometimes even longer). But in case of Tencent, I think the stock price performance is another indication of a great business model and a wide economic moat Tencent has around its business.

Tariffs and Uncertainty

Although we can be confident that Tencent is a great business with a wide economic moat, there are certainly risks surrounding the business and the level of uncertainty seems to be quite high.

In general – as argued several times in the past (for example in my last article about Tencent) – I see a huge risk for the United States to be headed for a recession, while China already was in a “recession” in the last few years and might be rather at the beginning of a new bull market.

Nevertheless, we must pay close attention to the trade dispute between the United States and China and what negative impacts tariffs will have on the global economy. A few days ago, it was announced that both countries slashed tariffs. However, the former reciprocal tariffs on Chinese goods of 145% are only suspended for 90 days and there is still a 30% tariff rates on Chinese goods imported to the United States and 10% tariff rate on goods imported to China. And of course, there is reason to be slightly optimistic but expectation stability, consistency or any form of predictability could be a huge mistake. The following chart (although the current tariff rate is probably not accurate anymore) is showing how huge the shift was that was happening in small steps already in the last few years and especially in April 2025.

While I think that the Chinese economy will be able to weather this storm and Tencent in particular is probably one of the companies not affected so heavily by the tariffs, I still think we should be cautious and take into account a scenario where the recovery of the Chinese economy will come to an abrupt end and growth rates for Tencent might decline steeply (or the company might also have to report declining revenue or declining earnings per share). Nevertheless, I remain rather optimistic for Tencent as the business is generating most of its revenue in China. Of course, we should not ignore International Games, which are growing with a high pace (23% in Q1/25) but the segment is responsible for less than 10% of total revenue.

Intrinsic Value Calculation

A final step we must include in every analysis is the calculation of an intrinsic value. Tencent is a great business, but that does not automatically mean the stock must be a great investment right now. Tencent is still trading about 33% below its all-time high and we might conclude that the stock is therefore a bargain. However, Tencent was probably overvalued in early 2021, when it was trading at its all-time high.

A much better way to determine if a stock is over- or undervalued is a discount cash flow calculation. As always, we are calculating with a 10% discount rate and the last reported number of shares outstanding, which is 9,261 million. I often take the free cash flow of the last four quarters as basis – in this case RMB 150.5 billion.

In a first rather conservative calculation, let’s assume 10% growth for the next ten years followed by 6% growth till perpetuity. When calculating with these assumptions we get an intrinsic value of RMB 539 (leading to HKD 584) and Tencent would therefore be only slightly undervalued.

However, we can also be a little more optimistic and assume 12% growth for the next ten years and once again 6% growth till perpetuity. This leads to an intrinsic value of RMB 621, and HKD 673 and we can call Tencent undervalued.

Conclusion

In my opinion, Tencent is still a “Buy” and the stock still seems to be undervalued and might be trading about 20% below its intrinsic value. Of course, the stock is not a screaming “Buy” and it might not be the best time to purchase the stock anymore. Additionally, the level of uncertainty is extremely high, and we don’t know whether geopolitics might have a huge negative impact on the economy (and Tencent as a business). Nevertheless, I think Tencent is one of the best businesses in the market and I am quite confident that the stock will trade higher in 5 and 10 years from now.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: JEPQ: A Yield Enhancing Investment With Exposure To Nasdaq-100 Companies
data.description:
Introduction

The JPMorgan Nasdaq Equity Premium Income ETF (NASDAQ:JEPQ) was established in May 2022. It is an actively managed ETF, which directly invests in U.S. large cap growth stocks, and more specifically, the Nasdaq-100 (QQQ) companies.

However, JEPQ is not a regular long-only ETF, which tracks a certain basket of stocks. On top of the long underlying exposure towards the Nasdaq-100 companies, JEPQ employs a covered call strategy. I will elaborate on this later in the article, but this nuance alone fundamentally changes the mechanics of how value and returns are generated.

Speaking of some general facts, there are couple of points that are worth mentioning.

JEPQ's assets under management (AUM) figure stands at ~$22 billion, which makes it one of the largest covered call ETFs out there. The JPMorgan Equity Premium Income ETF (NYSEARCA:JEPI) - a related fund, but with exposure towards the S&P 500 companies - has the larger AUM statistic compared to JEPQ, which is logical given the lower volatility (and thus risk) that is associated with the S&P 500.

However, such peers as NEOS NASDAQ-100 High Income ETF (NASDAQ:QQQI), REX FANG & Innovation Equity Premium Income ETF (NASDAQ:FEPI) and Global X Nasdaq-100 Covered Call ETF (NASDAQ:QYLD) have significantly smaller asset sizes.

This also translates to lower fees that JEPQ is able to provide. Even though JEPQ employs such complex processes to generate returns, it has a very low net expense ratio of just 0.35%. For example, QQQI, FEPI and QYLD have net expense ratios of 0.62%, 0.65% and 0.60%, respectively. Over time this kind of difference can compound to something meaningful, imposing a relative drag on the results.

Finally, since JEPQ invests in the Nasdaq-100 related stocks and then sells the index options against them, it means that there is no foreign exchange risk embedded in the ETF.

The Objective of JEPQ

The investment objective of JEPQ is to deliver monthly distributable income and Nasdaq-100 exposure with less volatility. It seeks current income while maintaining prospects for capital appreciation.

The design is calibrated to provide investors with performance that captures a majority of the Nasdaq-100 returns, while reducing the volatility. The design itself is also structured so that under normal circumstances, the monthly income streams remain stable.

In the chart below, we can see how JEPQ has performed since its inception compared to the Nasdaq-100 alternative.

There are a couple of takeaways.

First, about 40% of the total returns that JEPQ has generated over this time period has stemmed from the current income streams. It goes in line with JEPQ's primary strategy to produce attractive distributions.

Second, JEPQ has slightly underperformed the Nasdaq-100, which is logical given the rising markets and the drag that stems from the capped upside through the covered call strategy (more on that later).

Third, the swings in JEPQ's curve are lower, but not materially.

In the following chart we can see a similar pattern that the realized volatility for JEPQ during 2022, when the Fed started to aggressively hike interest rates, was correlated to the Nasdaq-100, but the registered total return loss was lower than for the Index.

This is a great example of how over a sustained period of elevated volatility and a relatively consistent downturn, JEPQ's option-driven defense comes into the play.

Mechanics of value creation

Turning to the process of how JEPQ works, it is important to distinguish the Nasdaq-100 factor, as it automatically introduces greater exposure towards high growth and technology stocks.

However, as briefly explained above, what JEPQ does on top of going long these QQQ stocks is it sells covered call options against them in an attempt to access premium income that could be then distributed to the shareholders.

This is the reason why JEPQ can offer, usually, double-digit dividend yield, while being exposed to the high growth, high-tech companies that tend to distribute small or no dividends.

All of the options are written on an out-of-the-money (OTM) basis, which means that their strike prices are above the prevailing market prices of the underlying portfolio securities (or rather the price level of the Nasdaq-100 index itself).

Having this kind of option overlay approach, completely changes the risk and return structure compared to a regular Nasdaq-100 investment or its underlying securities:

  • The sold call options generate a notable monthly income, where the key driver for how rich the premiums are is the volatility level in the Nasdaq-100 world.
  • The presence of the OTM options limit the upside potential for JEPQ. For example, if the Nasdaq-100 increases in price above the strike value that is associated with the written covered call options, then all these excess returns simply go past JEPQ shareholders. Instead, JEPQ shareholders remain limited to the collected option premiums and the price returns that come from the Nasdaq-100 index hitting the strike values.
  • In case of a downside move in the Nasdaq-100, JEPQ should outperform because of the pocketed option premiums.

Speaking of outperformance and differences from the Nasdaq-100, we have to keep in mind that JEPQ employs an applied data science approach to fundamental research and portfolio construction. In other words, JEPQ does not fully replicate the Nasdaq-100 index when selecting the underlying securities and determining their weights.

Here is a relevant excerpt from the Prospectus:

The adviser utilizes proprietary techniques to process, analyze, and combine a wide variety of data sources, including the adviser’s multi-decade history of proprietary fundamental research, company financial statements, and a variety of other data sources that the adviser finds relevant to conducting fundamental analysis. The adviser combines insights derived from these sources to forecast the financial prospects of each security, also known as fundamental analysis. Alongside its own insights, the Fund’s portfolio management team uses the forecasts developed through data science techniques to help to identify securities that are priced favorably relative to their associated levels of risk.

We can see visual differences when comparing the two tables below.

The Nasdaq-100 Top 10 holdings list:

JEPQ Top 10 holdings list:

While this is another aspect that leads to differences between the Nasdaq-100 and JEPQ returns, it is not as fundamental as the aforementioned option overlay strategy.

Key benefits

From the strategic and long-term investing perspective, JEPQ could benefit investor portfolios in the following ways:

  • Portfolio asset diversification as the Nasdaq-100 driven covered call strategy offers uncommon and unique risk and return exposures.
  • Portfolio income diversification for more dividend oriented investors, who typically source their current income streams from traditional value sectors such as midstream, tobacco, and utilities.
  • Portfolio yield enhancement, where JEPQ's double-digit dividend could boost the overall portfolio weighted average yield.

Key risks for JEPQ

There are two fundamental risks to JEPQ.

First, underperformance stemming from capped upside. As elaborated above, this is a natural consequence of the covered call strategy. For instance, if the Nasdaq-100 surges higher in a similar fashion, as it did in 2023 and 2024, the chances are high that we will see a similar level of negative alpha by JEPQ.

Second, high exposure to the richly priced and high-growth tilted companies in the underlying portfolio base. Even though JEPQ is better protected than a pure-play Nasdaq-100 exposure due to the collected option premiums, the lion's share of its portfolio will still be subject to significantly correlated drawdown as we saw in April 2025 when the tariff announcements rattled the market.

Summary (investor suitability)

In a nutshell, JEPQ is meant for income-oriented investors, who want to diversify their traditional value-tilted holdings with a Nasdaq-100 correlated risk and return factor. There is also a significant value of accessing low double-digit yield that in other alternative segments usually comes with either elevated credit risk or a high external leverage (think of closed end funds).

As elaborated above, JEPQ could be treated as a tactical investment play to benefit from elevated volatility levels and a likely range bound or slightly decreasing Nasdaq-100.

JEPQ is certainly not meant for investors who want to invest for long-term alpha and potentially sizeable returns that could be produced by the high-tech names. The capped upside from written option strike values does not allow JEPQ to fully participate in an upward trending market.

This article answers these three main questions about JEPQ:

  1. What types of investors is JEPQ suitable for?
  2. Does JEPQ's strategy reduce the volatility?
  3. Is JEPQ a good ETF for monthly income?

Editor's note: This article is intended to provide a general overview of the ETF for education purposes only and, unlike other articles on Seeking Alpha, does not offer an investment opinion about the ETF.

data.title: LMP Capital And Income Fund Inc. Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Performance Review

  • Despite a fast start to the year - a broad-based rally propelled the S&P 500 Index (SP500, SPX) +2.8% in January - the market struggled for traction in the first quarter of 2025. Post-election animal spirits faded in February and March as investors grappled with on again/off again tariff announcements and consternation over the AI outlook. By the end of the quarter, the S&P declined −4.3%, breaking the index's streak of five consecutive quarterly advances.
  • For the quarter, the fund returned −2.71% at NAV, while the benchmark, the 65% S&P 500,35% Bloomberg US Aggregate, returned −1.80%.

QUARTERLY KEY PERFORMANCE DRIVERS

  • Enterprise Products Partners LP (EPD), Plains GP Holdings LP (PAGP) and MPLX shares were higher as an energy-friendly administration took the White House and the trend for more power demand from AI-related data centers continued. Shares also rose as the energy sector outperformed amid rising oil and gas prices.
  • Broadcom (AVGO) was lower as the unveiling of Chinese AI startup DEEPSEEK, whose new foundational and reasoning models - reportedly offering similar performance to leading U.S. models at lower compute costs - triggered a broader selloff in the AI ecosystem.
  • Blue Owl Capital (OBDC) and Apollo Asset Management (APO) 6.75% convertible preferred shares were down as the market rotated from alternative asset managers, which performed well in 2024.

Outlook & Strategy

  • Market risks remain elevated as a result of anemic economic activity due to still-high interest rates, tight financial conditions and heightened political uncertainty, particularly in the areas of tariffs, taxes and fiscal spending priorities. The cloud of unpredictability and uncertainty surrounding these topics is causing elevated volatility on an almost daily basis. The war in the Middle East is nearing its final stages and the risk to the overall market appears to be contained. The U.S. housing market remains weak, in large part due to still-high mortgage rates and weakening economic fundamentals. Corporate earnings are solid but remain under pressure from a slowing economy and high interest rates, which puts upward pressure on interest expense. We believe the Federal Reserve will pause its rate decreases for the time being, but we continue to anticipate additional cuts in 2025, which we view as a positive for the economy more broadly and risk assets such as stocks in particular.
  • As always, we carefully weigh short-term risks against the longer-term backdrop, and our assessment is that the environment remains challenging for risk assets such as common stocks in the short term. In the long term, we continue to be comfortable owning high-quality companies that exhibit leadership positions in their sectors, have sound balance sheets, generate strong free cash flow and offer attractive dividend yields and dividend growth potential. In particular, we seek out and analyze those companies that can represent the next generation of dividend aristocrats and have identified a number of our existing holdings that could reach that status over time.
  • In recognizing the current volatile investment landscape, we have been increasing our positions in defensive names as evidenced by our recent initiations in Walmart (WMT) and PepsiCo (PEP) in consumer staples and McKesson (MCK) in health care. Indeed, we have increased our focus on more defensive sectors like consumer staples and health care. Those sectors are also very good hunting grounds to find high-quality (emerging) dividend aristocrats, which typically offer solid dividend profiles while providing more stability during turbulent times. By the same token, we have diminished our positions in more cyclical sectors such as information technology and alternative asset managers and used the proceeds as a source of funds for these more defensive companies. We continue to carefully assess the risks and rewards in our exposures and recognize that the investment environment has become increasingly volatile.
  • We remain opportunistic and tactical in managing through the high level of volatility in the energy market. Oil supply remains adequate for the time being and the risk of escalation of the war in the Middle East involving oil-related infrastructure such as those in Iran appears diminished. We continue to emphasize balance sheet strength, asset footprint diversity and quality, and we carefully assess our holdings with an eye toward managing successfully through the short-term disruption and thriving in the long term. We continue to believe that in the long term, midstream companies and MLPs continue to represent an attractive investment opportunity as the U.S. continues to cement its status as an energy superpower and exhibit sustained hydrocarbon production increases, which can bode well for high-quality energy MLPs as volumes to be processed are poised to increase over time.
  • With relative weakness in the REIT sector over the past year, largely a result of high interest rates, we continue to assess whether our exposure warrants a change. For the time being, we are content holding onto to our positions. As always, we emphasize those REITs with strong balance sheets, ample cash flows, leadership positions in their sectors and attractive valuations. In particular, we continue to hold infrastructure REITs such as data center REITs and telecommunication REITs where growth opportunities remain robust due to the buildout of artificial intelligence and cloud computing. We believe these next-generation REITs are well-positioned to capitalize on this large opportunity ahead.
  • We continue to believe alternative asset managers are an attractive long-term opportunity as many institutional investors seek uncorrelated returns, which these alternative asset managers can provide. Further, they operate in a loosely regulated environment, unlike their large bank counterparties, providing a competitive and sustainable advantage. We particularly like the sector leaders and we expect the growth of assets under management for them to continue to increase.
  • We remain bullish about the growth prospects for our technology holdings, especially those holdings that have the prospect for outsize growth opportunities in the artificial intelligence and cloud computing ecosystem. This includes hyperscalers, select software companies, semiconductor investments and some energy and utility companies. In our view, the portfolio is well-positioned to capitalize on these trends, which we believe to be large and long term in nature.
